feynman.0: Gravity, electricity, magnetism — here are three forces that reach across empty space and push or pull on things they never touch. How do they pull that off? The trick is the field. The idea is this: every single point in space is carrying a little vector around with it, and the moment you drop an object there, that object feels the matching force. Get this one model straight and the next four topics are really just it, dressed up four different ways.
feynman.1: A field is a region of space where an object feels a force without anything touching it. Let me say it more carefully: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space — one value per point, everywhere. Picture a temperature map of Victoria: that’s a scalar field, just one number sitting at each spot. But the three fields we care about here — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are vector fields: at every point the field carries both a size and a direction.
feynman.2: How do you find out what the field is doing at some point? You poke it with a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. The catch is that your test object has to be small enough not to shove the very field you’re trying to measure. Then field strength is just the force the field puts on that test object, divided by however much of the property the field grabs onto.
feynman.3: Each field comes with its own rule for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points the way the field pushes a test mass — and that’s always toward the source mass. The electric field points the way the field pushes a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points the way the north pole of a compass needle ends up pointing.
feynman.4: That old friend g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ from Units 1&2 was a field strength all along: down near Earth’s surface the gravitational force on every kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ is just telling you exactly that — newtons per kilogram. Magnetic field strength B gets measured in tesla (T), and it’s pinned down through the force on a moving charge, which we’ll dig into in 7 · Magnetic fields.
feynman.5: Key idea A field has a value — a size and a direction — at every point in space, whether or not there’s a test object sitting there. The test object just lets you see the field; it doesn’t make it.
feynman.6: You can’t draw a separate little arrow at every point — there are infinitely many — so instead we draw vector fields as field lines: smooth, continuous curves whose direction at any point along them is the field’s direction right there. Four rules keep every honest field diagram in line.
feynman.7: Take a look at FIG 1.1: the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges fan out radially. Around any point source the same bunch of lines has to spread over a bigger and bigger area as you back away, so the lines get farther apart and the field gets weaker. That right there is where the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6 come from.
feynman.8: Trap Field lines are a way of drawing the field, not actual objects sitting in space. Stick a test mass in the gap between two drawn lines and it still feels a force: the field fills up all of space, and how many lines you choose to draw is just a matter of bookkeeping.
feynman.9: The three fields have a lot in common — a vector at every point, a force on the right kind of object, and a strength you can read off from how crowded the lines are — but they part ways on where they come from and on whether they can push as well as pull. The table lays the comparison side by side, the way the study design asks for it.
feynman.10: A monopole is a place where field lines are born or where they die: they all point away from it, or they all point toward it, but never both. A dipole is one of each paired up — a source and a sink — with lines running from one over to the other. Gravity only ever has the one kind of monopole, because masses only pull, never push. Electric charge comes in two signs, so electricity gets both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism, on the other hand, gets dipoles and nothing but.
feynman.11: Look at FIG 1.2: the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet have exactly the same shape. The difference is in what you’re allowed to pull apart. You can yank the two charges of an electric dipole apart and end up with two separate monopoles — but cut a bar magnet in half and all you get is two smaller dipoles. You never, ever get a lone pole.
feynman.12: Trap Chopping a bar magnet in two does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half comes out a complete dipole, with its own N and its own S — and this keeps going all the way down, right to the level of individual electrons.
feynman.13: Key idea Field lines head away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism has both, at every point, and you can’t pry them apart.
feynman.14: Magnetic fields come from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. Back in 1820 Ørsted spotted a compass needle twitch when it sat near a wire carrying current — the first hint anyone had that moving charge makes a magnetic field. Just to give you a sense of scale, Earth’s magnetic field here in Melbourne runs about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
feynman.15: A bar magnet makes the dipole field you saw in FIG 1.2: lines on the outside running from N to S, packed tightest at the poles. A straight wire makes something different — circular field lines wrapped around the wire, with no poles anywhere at all. To get the direction, use the right-hand grip rule: grab the wire with your right hand, thumb pointing along the conventional current, and your fingers curl the way the magnetic field goes. The circles spread out as you move away from the wire, which is the field telling you it’s getting weaker.
feynman.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field from every little segment gets funnelled the same way through the middle, so the field piles up there; the two faces of the loop start acting like N and S poles. A solenoid — just lots of loops sitting side by side — adds all those contributions together: inside, the field is strong and beautifully uniform; outside, it looks just like a bar magnet’s. The grip rule bends to fit: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As FIG 1.3 shows, the N end is where the field lines come out.
feynman.17: Fields live in three dimensions, but pages are flat, so we cheat with a convention: × means a vector going into the page, and • means one coming out of the page. You’ll get plenty of practice with this notation in 7 · Magnetic fields.
feynman.18: Trap The grip rule runs on conventional current — the direction positive charge flows, out of the + terminal, around through the circuit, and back to the −. Point your thumb along the electron flow instead and you’ll predict a field that’s backwards by a full 180°.
feynman.19: Every field can be sorted along two separate axes. A field is static if its size and direction at each point just sit there and don’t change with time, and changing if they do. A bar magnet sitting still, a charged sphere bolted in place, a solenoid carrying a steady current — all of those make static fields; spin that magnet, or run alternating current through that coil, and now you’ve got a changing field.
feynman.20: A field is uniform across some region if it has the same size and the same direction at every point in there — which you draw as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It’s non-uniform if either one wanders from point to point. The field between two oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As FIG 1.4 shows, Earth’s gravitational field is non-uniform once you zoom out to the size of the planet — yet inside a single laboratory the lines run so nearly parallel that we just treat the field as uniform, at a steady 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
feynman.21: Key idea Uniform versus non-uniform is about how the field changes as you move through space; static versus changing is about how it changes as time ticks by. The two sortings have nothing to do with each other, and every field walks away wearing one label from each pair.
feynman.22: Connects That static-versus-changing distinction takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field will induce an emf in a coil. A generator is built for exactly one job — to manufacture that change.
feynman.23: Connects Unit 4 pushes the field idea even further: a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, and that makes a changing electric field, and so on — the self-sustaining pair goes sailing off through empty space as light, a field that’s cut loose from any source at all.

sagan.0: Gravity, electricity and magnetism reach across emptiness and pull or push on things they never touch — and for a long time this seemed almost like magic. The field model is how we tame that mystery: imagine that every point in space, even where nothing at all appears to be, quietly carries a vector, and any object set down at that point feels the corresponding force. This is the idea that the next four topics will lean upon.
sagan.1: A field is a region of space in which an object feels a force without anything reaching out to touch it. Said more carefully, a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every single point in space. Picture a temperature map of Victoria: one number hovering over each town — that is a scalar field, a value but no direction. The three fields at the heart of this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are richer things, vector fields: at every point each one carries both a magnitude and a direction.
sagan.2: To coax the value at a point into view, we bring a test object: a small mass to probe the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. The test object must be small enough that it whispers rather than shouts — small enough that it does not disturb the very field it has come to measure. Field strength is then simply the force the field exerts on that test object, reckoned per unit of the property the field acts upon.
sagan.3: Each field keeps to its own convention of direction. The gravitational field at a point points the way the field would push a test mass — always inward, toward the source mass that summons it. The electric field points the way the field would push a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points wherever the north pole of a compass needle chooses to face.
sagan.4: That familiar number from Units 1&2, g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹, was a field strength all along: near Earth's surface the gravitational force pressing on each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says precisely that, force per unit mass. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is defined through the force felt by a moving charge, a story told in 7 · Magnetic fields.
sagan.5: Key idea A field has a value — both magnitude and direction — at every point in space, whether or not any test object happens to be there to feel it. The test object only reveals the field; it does not bring the field into being.
sagan.6: Drawing a separate little arrow at every point in space is hopeless — there are infinitely many — so instead we draw vector fields as field lines, continuous curves that flow always in the direction the field points at each place they pass. Four simple rules govern every field diagram drawn correctly.
sagan.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the patterns around a point mass and around point charges spread outward in straight spokes — they are radial. From any point source the very same set of lines must fan out across an ever larger area as distance grows, so the lines drift apart and the field grows weaker. This simple geometry is the seed of the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
sagan.8: Trap Field lines are a picture we draw, not real threads strung through space. A test mass set down in the apparently empty gap between two drawn lines still feels a force, because the field fills all of space without a single gap, and how many lines we choose to draw is merely a matter of convention.
sagan.9: The three fields are kin — each carries a vector at every point, each exerts a force on its own kind of object, and each shows its strength through the crowding of its lines — yet they differ in where they come from and in whether they can push as well as pull. The table gathers the comparison the study design asks of you.
sagan.10: A monopole is a single source or a single sink of field lines: the lines stream away from it, or they stream toward it, but never both at once. A dipole is a source and a sink bound together, with lines arcing from the one to the other. Gravity knows monopoles of only one kind, for masses can only ever attract, never repel. Electric charge comes in two signs, so electricity enjoys both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism, curiously, has dipoles and nothing else.
sagan.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet trace the very same shape. The difference is hidden in what can be torn apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be pulled clean away into two separate monopoles, but slice a bar magnet in half and you are rewarded with two smaller dipoles — never a lone, isolated pole.
sagan.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in two does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half emerges as a complete dipole with its own N and its own S — and this stubborn refusal continues all the way down, down to the level of the individual electron.
sagan.13: Key idea Field lines flow away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity offers sinks alone. Electricity offers both sources and sinks. Magnetism carries both at every point, forever inseparable.
sagan.14: Magnetic fields are born of two kinds of source: permanent magnets, and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted happened to notice a compass needle twitch aside near a current-carrying wire — the first hint humanity ever had that moving charge conjures a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, Earth's own magnetic field here in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
sagan.15: A bar magnet spins out the dipole field of FIG 1.2: lines streaming outside from N to S, crowding thickest at the poles. A straight wire does something stranger — it wraps the space around itself in circular field lines, with no poles anywhere at all. To find their direction, reach for the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire with your right hand, thumb pointing along the conventional current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. The circles loosen and spread with distance from the wire, showing the field fading away.
sagan.16: Bend that wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment is made to thread the same way through the centre, so the field there gathers and concentrates; the two faces of the loop now behave as an N pole and an S pole. A solenoid — many such loops set side by side — pools all these contributions together: inside, the field runs strong and uniform; outside, it mimics the field of a bar magnet. The grip rule adapts to suit: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current circles the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is simply where the field lines stream out.
sagan.17: Three-dimensional fields must somehow be pinned to a flat page, so we borrow a convention: × for a vector plunging into the page, and • for a vector emerging out of it. This little notation is practised throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
sagan.18: Trap The grip rule speaks the language of conventional current — the direction in which positive charge flows, from the + terminal out through the circuit and back to the −. Aim your thumb along the electron flow instead and you will predict a field pointing exactly the wrong way, reversed by a full 180°.
sagan.19: Every field can be placed on two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold perfectly still as time passes, and changing if they do not. A bar magnet sitting motionless, a charged sphere held fixed, a solenoid carrying a steady current — each pours out a static field; set the magnet spinning, or feed the coil an alternating current, and the field begins to change.
sagan.20: A field is uniform across a region if it has the same magnitude and the same direction at every point within it — drawn as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It is non-uniform if either of those wavers from place to place. The field in the gap between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field surrounding a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As shown in FIG 1.4, Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform on the grand planetary scale, and yet within the small confines of a laboratory the lines run so very nearly parallel that we treat the field as uniform, at a steady 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
sagan.21: Key idea Uniform and non-uniform describe how a field varies across space; static and changing describe how it varies through time. The two classifications are entirely independent, and every field in the universe wears one label drawn from each pair.
sagan.22: Connects The static-versus-changing distinction moves to centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field can induce an emf in a coil. A generator is built for no other reason than to manufacture that change.
sagan.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, and that to another changing electric field, and so on — the self-sustaining pair sailing onward through empty space as light itself, a field that has cut loose from any source and travels free.

curie.1: A field is a region of space in which an object experiences a non-contact force. More precisely, a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. cox.0: Gravity, electricity and magnetism all reach across empty space and push or pull without ever touching. The wonderful thing is that we can say how: imagine that every point in space carries a little arrow, a vector, and that anything you place there feels the force that arrow describes. This topic builds that one idea — and the next four topics lean on it entirely.
cox.1: A field is a region of space in which an object feels a non-contact force. Put more carefully, a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every single point in space. Think of a temperature map of Victoria: one number at each place — that's a scalar field. The three fields in this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are richer than that. They are vector fields: at every point the field carries both a magnitude and a direction.
cox.2: To find out what the field is doing at a point, you bring along a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. It has to be small — small enough that it doesn't disturb the very field you're trying to measure. Field strength is then simply the force the field exerts on that test object, per unit of whatever property the field acts on.
cox.3: Each field has its own quiet rule for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points along the force the field exerts on a test mass — and that's always toward the source mass. The electric field points along the force the field exerts on a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points the way the north pole of a compass needle turns to face.
cox.4: That familiar g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ from Units 1&2 is a field strength all along: near Earth's surface, the gravitational force on each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that, no more and no less. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T), and it's defined through the force on a moving charge, which we explore in 7 · Magnetic fields.
cox.5: Key idea A field has a value — a magnitude and a direction — at every point in space, whether or not a test object happens to be there. The test object reveals the field; it does not bring it into being.
cox.6: You couldn't possibly draw a separate arrow at every point — there are infinitely many. So instead we draw vector fields as field lines: continuous curves whose direction, at every point along them, is the field's direction there. And four simple rules govern every correct field diagram.
cox.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges are radial — they spread straight outward like spokes. Around any point source, that same set of lines spreads over a larger and larger area as you move away, so the spacing opens up and the field grows weaker. This, beautifully, is the root of the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
cox.8: Trap Field lines are a way of picturing things, not physical objects. A test mass set down in the gap between two drawn lines still feels a force: the field fills all of space, and how many lines we choose to draw is just a matter of convention.
cox.9: The three fields share a deep family resemblance — a vector at every point, a force on the appropriate kind of object, and a strength you can read off from how densely the lines crowd together — but they differ in their sources, and in whether they can push as well as pull. The table gathers the comparison the study design asks for.
cox.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines: the lines stream away from it, or in toward it, but never both at once. A dipole is a source and a sink paired up, with lines running across from one to the other. Gravity has monopoles of one kind only, because masses can only ever attract. Electric charge comes in two signs, so electricity has both monopoles and dipoles; and magnetism, curiously, has dipoles only.
cox.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet have exactly the same shape. The difference lies in what you can pull apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be separated into two monopoles, but cut a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller dipoles — never an isolated pole, no matter how you try.
cox.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half is a complete dipole, with its own N and S — and this keeps going all the way down to the level of individual electrons.
cox.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and in toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism has both, at every point, and you can never prise them apart.
cox.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted noticed a compass needle twitch as it sat beside a current-carrying wire — the very first sign that moving charge makes a magnetic field. To give you a sense of scale, Earth's magnetic field here in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
cox.15: A bar magnet makes the dipole field of FIG 1.2: external lines sweeping from N to S, crowding most densely at the poles. A straight wire makes circular field lines wrapped around the wire, with no poles at all. To find which way they go, use the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire with your right hand, thumb pointing along the conventional current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. The circles spread apart as you move away from the wire, showing the field fading.
cox.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment threads the same way through the centre, so the field there is concentrated; the two faces of the loop behave as N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops nestled side by side — adds all these contributions together: inside, the field is strong and beautifully uniform; outside, it looks just like a bar magnet's field. The grip rule adapts neatly: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is where the field lines stream out.
cox.17: Three-dimensional fields have to be drawn on flat pages, so we use a lovely little convention: × for a vector heading into the page and • for one coming out toward you. You'll get plenty of practice with this throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
cox.18: Trap The grip rule uses conventional current — the direction positive charge flows, from the + terminal round through the circuit to the −. Point your thumb along the electron flow instead, and you'll predict a field direction flipped a full 180°.
cox.19: Every field can be sorted along two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold steady over time, and changing if they don't. A bar magnet sitting still, a charged sphere held fixed, a solenoid carrying a steady current — all make static fields; a spinning magnet, or a coil carrying alternating current, makes a changing one.
cox.20: A field is uniform across a region if it has the same magnitude and the same direction at every point there — drawn as parallel, equally spaced lines. It's non-uniform if either of those varies from place to place. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As shown in FIG 1.4, Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform on the planetary scale, and yet within a single laboratory the lines are so very nearly parallel that we happily treat the field as uniform, at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
cox.21: Key idea Uniform/non-uniform tells you about variation across space; static/changing tells you about variation over time. The two classifications are quite independent, and every field carries one label from each pair.
cox.22: Connects That static/changing distinction takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field induces an emf in a coil. A generator is built, from the ground up, to manufacture exactly that change.
cox.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, and that in turn to a changing electric field — and the self-sustaining pair travels through empty space as light, a field with no source attached to it at all.

Imagine that every point in space quietly carries a vector, an instruction waiting to be obeyed—and any object placed at that point feels the force that instruction prescribes. This topic builds the picture that the next four topics will lean on.
greene.1: A field is a region of space in which an object feels a non-contact force—but let us be more precise. A field is a physical quantity that takes a value at every single point in space. Think of a temperature map of Victoria: at each point there sits one number, and nothing more. That is a scalar field. The three fields at the heart of this area of study—gravitational, electric and magnetic—are richer creatures. They are vector fields: at every point the field carries not just a magnitude but a direction as well.

greene.3: Each field carries its own convention for which way it points. The electric field points in the direction of the force the field exerts on a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points in the direction the north pole of a compass needle chooses to face.
greene.4: That familiar companion from Units 1&2, g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹, is nothing other than a field strength: near Earth's surface the gravitational force pressing on each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says precisely that, in so many letters. greene.6: Drawing a separate little arrow at every one of the infinitely many points in space is hopeless, so we draw vector fields instead as field lines—continuous curves whose direction at every point traces the field's direction there. Four rules govern every correct field diagram.
greene.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the patterns woven around a point mass and around point charges are radial. greene.8: Trap Field lines are a representation, never physical objects in their own right. A test mass set down in the gap between two drawn lines still feels a force: the field fills all of space without interruption, and the number of lines we choose to draw is purely a matter of convention.
greene.9: The three fields share a common architecture—a vector at every point, a force exerted on the appropriate kind of object, and a strength betrayed by how densely the lines crowd together—yet they part ways in their sources, and in whether they can repel as well as attract. The table gathers the comparison the study design asks of you.

The difference hides in what can be pried apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be separated into two lone monopoles, but slice a bar magnet in half and you are rewarded with two smaller dipoles—never an isolated pole.
greene.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half emerges as a complete dipole with its own N and S—and this stubborn truth continues all the way down, to the level of individual electrons.
greene.13: Key idea Field lines stream away from sources and inward toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism carries both at every point, inseparably bound together.
greene.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. greene.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment is made to thread the same way through the centre, so the field there is concentrated; the two faces of the loop come to act as N and S poles. greene.22: Connects The static/changing distinction takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field can induce an emf in a coil. A generator is built, from the ground up, to manufacture that change.
greene.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives birth to a changing magnetic field, and that in turn to a changing electric field—and the self-sustaining pair sails off through empty space as light, a field with no source attached to it at all.

The electric field points the same way as the force on a positive test charge. The magnetic field points whichever way the north pole of a compass needle points.

Near Earth's surface the gravitational force on each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that and nothing more: newtons, per kilogram. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is defined through the force on a moving charge, which is worked out in 7 · Magnetic fields.
munroe.5: Key idea A field has a value — a size and a direction — at every point in space, whether or not a test object is there to feel it. The test object reveals the field. It does not create it. The field was already there.
munroe.6: Drawing a separate arrow at every point is impossible — there are infinitely many points and one page — so vector fields get drawn as field lines instead: continuous curves whose direction at any point along them is the field's direction right there. Four rules govern every correct field diagram.
munroe.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges are radial: lines straight out, like spokes. Around any point source, that same set of lines has to cover a bigger and bigger area as you go out, so the lines spread apart and the field gets weaker. That spreading-out is the whole reason behind the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
munroe.8: Trap Field lines are a way of drawing the field, not actual objects sitting in space. A test mass placed in the gap between two drawn lines still feels a force — the field is everywhere, it fills all of space, and the number of lines you chose to draw is just a matter of convention. The field doesn't skip the gaps.
munroe.9: The three fields have a lot in common: a vector at every point, a force on the appropriate kind of object, and strength shown by how tightly the lines pack together. Where they part ways is in what produces them, and in whether they can push as well as pull. The table lays out the comparison the study design asks for.
munroe.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines: the lines either all point away from it or all point toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink paired up, with the lines running across from one to the other. Gravity only has monopoles of one kind, because masses only ever attract — there is no anti-mass that pushes. Electric charge comes in two signs, so electricity gets both monopoles and dipoles. Magnetism only ever gives you dipoles.
munroe.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet look exactly the same shape. The difference is what you're allowed to take apart. The two charges of an electric dipole can be pulled apart into two separate monopoles, one positive and one negative. Cut a bar magnet in half, though, and you get two smaller dipoles — never a lone north pole sitting on its own.
munroe.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half is a complete dipole, with its own N and S. Do it again and the same thing happens. This keeps going all the way down, right to the level of individual electrons.
munroe.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism has both as well, but at every point, and you can't pry them apart.
munroe.14: Magnetic fields come from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted happened to notice that a compass needle twitches when it's near a current-carrying wire — the first sign that moving charge makes a magnetic field, and nobody had been looking for it. For a sense of scale, Earth's magnetic field in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
munroe.15: A bar magnet produces the dipole field of FIG 1.2: lines running outside from N to S, packed tightest at the poles. A straight wire produces circular field lines wrapped around the wire, with no poles anywhere at all. To find which way they go, use the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire with your right hand, thumb pointing the way the conventional current flows, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. The circles get further apart as you move away from the wire, which is the field weakening.
munroe.16: Bend the wire into a loop and now the circular field of every bit of the wire passes the same way through the centre, so the field there piles up; the two faces of the loop end up acting as an N pole and an S pole. A solenoid — a stack of loops side by side — adds all of those up: inside, the field is strong and uniform; outside, it looks just like a bar magnet's field. The grip rule still works, you just point it differently: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current goes around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is the end where the field lines come out.
munroe.17: A field in three dimensions has to be drawn on a flat page, so there's a convention for arrows pointing straight at you or straight away: × for a vector going into the page, • for a vector coming out of it. Think of the tail feathers of an arrow flying away versus the tip coming toward you. This notation gets plenty of use in 7 · Magnetic fields.
munroe.18: Trap The grip rule uses conventional current — the direction positive charge would flow, from the + terminal around through the circuit to the −. If you point your thumb along the electron flow instead, you'll predict a field that points the wrong way by 180°, i.e. exactly backwards.
munroe.19: Every field can be sorted along two separate axes. A field is static if its size and direction at each point stay put over time, and changing if they don't. A bar magnet sitting still, a charged sphere bolted in place, and a solenoid carrying a steady current all produce static fields. A magnet that's spinning, or a coil carrying alternating current, produces a changing one.
munroe.20: A field is uniform in some region if it has the same size and the same direction at every point in that region — drawn as parallel lines, equally spaced. It's non-uniform if either one varies from point to point. The field between two oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As shown in FIG 1.4, Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform if you zoom out to the whole planet, but inside a single laboratory the lines are so close to parallel that you just treat the field as uniform at 9.8 N kg⁻¹ and don't lose any sleep over it.
munroe.21: Key idea Uniform versus non-uniform is about whether the field varies across space; static versus changing is about whether it varies over time. The two are completely independent, and every field carries exactly one label from each pair.
munroe.22: Connects The static/changing distinction becomes the whole point in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field induces an emf in a coil. A steady one does nothing. A generator is built for the express purpose of producing that change.
munroe.23: Connects Unit 4 pushes the field idea even further: a changing electric field produces a changing magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field produces a changing electric field, and the self-sustaining pair takes off through empty space as light — a field with no source attached to it at all.

bronowski.0: Gravity, electricity, magnetism — each reaches across empty space and pulls or pushes on a body that touches nothing at all. How can a force act where there is no contact? The field model is the answer humankind arrived at: imagine that every point in space already carries a vector, a quiet instruction waiting, so that an object set down there feels at once the force appointed to that place. This topic builds that model, and the next four topics live inside it.
bronowski.1: A field, in its plainest sense, is a region of space where an object feels a force without anything touching it. But the deeper meaning is this: a field is a physical quantity that possesses a value at every single point in space. Spread a temperature map across Victoria and you have a scalar field — one bare number at each place. The three fields of this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are of a richer kind, vector fields: at every point the field holds not only a magnitude but a direction as well.
bronowski.2: To read the value at a point we send in a test object: a small mass to question the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric, a compass needle for the magnetic. It must be small — small enough that it listens without disturbing, that it does not bend the very field it has come to measure. Field strength, then, is the force the field lays upon this test object, reckoned per unit of whatever property the field acts upon.
bronowski.3: Each field keeps its own convention of direction. The gravitational field at a point lies along the force the field exerts on a test mass — and that is always inward, toward the source mass. The electric field lies along the force exerted on a positive test charge. The magnetic field lies in the direction the north pole of a compass needle chooses to point.
bronowski.4: That familiar figure carried up from Units 1&2, g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹, was a field strength all along: near the surface of the Earth the gravitational force laid upon each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says precisely that and nothing more. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T), and it is defined through the force exerted on a moving charge, a matter taken up in 7 · Magnetic fields.
bronowski.5: Key idea A field has its value — magnitude and direction — at every point in space, whether or not any test object is there to feel it. The test object reveals the field; it does not bring it into being.
bronowski.6: To draw a separate arrow at every point is beyond us, for the points are without number. So we render a vector field as field lines — continuous curves whose direction, at every point along them, is the direction of the field there. Four rules govern every honest field diagram.
bronowski.7: As FIG 1.1 shows, the patterns woven around a point mass and around point charges are radial. Out from any point source the same set of lines must spread across an ever-larger area as it travels, so the lines thin apart and the field grows weaker. Here is the root of the inverse-square laws we shall meet in topics 5 and 6.
bronowski.8: Trap Field lines are a way of seeing, not things in themselves. A test mass laid in the empty space between two drawn lines still feels a force: the field fills all of space without gap, and the number of lines we choose to draw is only a matter of convention.
bronowski.9: The three fields share a common architecture — a vector at every point, a force exerted on the proper kind of object, a strength betrayed by the crowding of the lines — yet they part company in their sources, and in whether they can push away as well as draw in. The table sets out the comparison the study design asks of you.
bronowski.10: A monopole is a source or else a sink of field lines: the lines stream away from it, or they stream toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink bound together as a pair, with the lines running across from the one to the other. Gravity owns monopoles of a single kind only, for mass can do nothing but attract. Electric charge comes in two signs, and so electricity has both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism has dipoles, and dipoles alone.
bronowski.11: As FIG 1.2 shows, the field of an electric dipole and the field reaching outside a bar magnet wear the very same shape. The difference is hidden in what may be parted: the two charges of an electric dipole can be drawn asunder into two separate monopoles, but cut a bar magnet through the middle and you have made two smaller dipoles, never a lone and solitary pole.
bronowski.12: Trap Cut a bar magnet in half and you do not come away with one north piece in one hand and one south piece in the other. Each half is a whole dipole entire, with its own N and its own S — and this goes on down, halving after halving, to the level of the single electron itself.
bronowski.13: Key idea Field lines stream away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks and nothing else. Electricity has sources and sinks both. Magnetism has both at every point, and they cannot be torn apart.
bronowski.14: Magnetic fields rise from two kinds of source: permanent magnets, and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted saw a compass needle swing aside near a wire that carried a current — the first sign mankind ever had that moving charge gives birth to a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, the Earth's magnetic field in Melbourne stands at about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
bronowski.15: A bar magnet casts the dipole field of FIG 1.2: the outer lines run from N to S, crowding thickest at the poles. A straight wire casts circular field lines wheeling about the wire, with no poles whatsoever. Their direction is given by the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire in the right hand, the thumb laid along the conventional current; the fingers curl the way the magnetic field goes. The circles loosen apart with distance from the wire, and in that loosening you read the field's decline.
bronowski.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment is made to thread the same way through the centre, so there the field gathers and concentrates; the two faces of the loop now stand as N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops ranged side by side — gathers all these contributions together: within, the field is strong and uniform; without, it is the very image of a bar magnet's field. The grip rule bends to suit: curl the fingers of the right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and the thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As FIG 1.3 shows, the N end is the place where the field lines come out.
bronowski.17: Three-dimensional fields must be set down on flat pages, and for this we use the convention × for a vector going into the page and • for a vector coming out of it. The notation is practised throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
bronowski.18: Trap The grip rule reckons by conventional current — the direction in which positive charge flows, out from the + terminal, through the circuit, and home to the −. Lay the thumb instead along the flow of the electrons and you will predict a field turned the wrong way, reversed by a full 180°.
bronowski.19: Every field may be sorted along two axes that stand quite apart from one another. A field is static if its magnitude and its direction at each point hold fast and do not change with time, and changing if they do not. A bar magnet at rest, a fixed charged sphere, a solenoid carrying a steady current — all of these pour out static fields; a magnet set spinning, or a coil fed with alternating current, pours out a changing one.
bronowski.20: A field is uniform through a region if it holds the same magnitude and the same direction at every point within it — and we draw it as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It is non-uniform if either one shifts from place to place. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field about a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As FIG 1.4 shows, the Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform when seen across the whole planet, yet within the walls of a laboratory the lines run so nearly parallel that we may treat the field as uniform at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
bronowski.21: Key idea Uniform and non-uniform speak of how a field varies across space; static and changing speak of how it varies through time. The two classifications are wholly independent, and every field bears one label drawn from each pair.
bronowski.22: Connects The static-and-changing distinction comes into its own in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field will induce an emf in a coil. A generator is nothing but a machine built to manufacture that change.
bronowski.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, and that to the electric again, and the self-sustaining pair travels off through empty space as light — a field that has cut loose from any source at all.

mack.0: Gravity, electricity and magnetism all reach across empty space and push or pull on things they never touch. This topic builds the model — and the next four topics lean on it the whole way.

The catch is that the test object has to be small enough that it doesn’t go disturbing the very field you’re trying to measure. mack.3: Each field comes with its own rule for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points along the force the field exerts on a test mass — which is always straight toward the source mass. The electric field points along the force the field exerts on a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points whichever way the north pole of a compass needle swings to face.
mack.4: That trusty g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ you met back in Units 1&2 is a field strength, plain and simple: near Earth’s surface the gravitational force on every kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that out loud. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is pinned down through the force on a moving charge — which gets the full treatment in 7 · Magnetic fields.
mack.5: Key idea A field has a value — both magnitude and direction — at every point in space, whether or not a test object happens to be sitting there. The test object reveals the field; it doesn’t conjure it into being.
mack.6: Drawing a separate little arrow at every single point is hopeless, so we draw vector fields as field lines instead — continuous curves that point, at every point along them, the way the field points there. Four rules keep every honest field diagram in line.
mack.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges fan out radially. Around any point source the same handful of lines has to spread across a bigger and bigger area as you move out, so the lines drift apart and the field fades. This is the seed of the inverse-square laws coming up in topics 5 and 6.
mack.8: Trap Field lines are a drawing, not actual objects. A test mass parked in the gap between two drawn lines still feels a force — the field fills all of space, and how many lines you choose to draw is just a matter of convention.
mack.9: The three fields have a lot in common — a vector at every point, a force on the right kind of object, strength shown by how tightly packed the lines are — but they part ways on where they come from and on whether they can ever push as well as pull. The table lays out the comparison the study design asks you to know.
mack.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines all on its own: the lines stream away from it, or they stream toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink paired up, with lines running across from one to the other. Gravity only ever has monopoles of a single kind, because masses can only pull. Electric charge comes in two flavours, so electricity gets both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism, oddly enough, gets dipoles only.
mack.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet trace out the very same shape. Where they differ is in what you can pull apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be peeled away into two separate monopoles, but slice a bar magnet down the middle and you get two smaller dipoles — never a lone pole.
mack.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half comes out as a complete dipole with its own N and S — and this keeps right on going, all the way down to individual electrons.
mack.13: Key idea Field lines stream away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has sources and sinks. Magnetism has both, at every point, and you can’t prise them apart.
mack.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted spotted a compass needle twitch as it passed a current-carrying wire — the first hint that moving charge makes a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, Earth’s magnetic field in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
mack.15: A bar magnet makes the dipole field of FIG 1.2: external lines arcing from N to S, packed tightest at the poles. A straight wire makes circular field lines that wrap around it, with no poles at all. To find which way they wrap, reach for the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire in your right hand with the thumb pointing along the conventional current, and your fingers curl the way the magnetic field goes. The circles loosen out the further you get from the wire — the field fading as you leave.
mack.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field from every segment all threads the same way through the centre, so the field bunches up there; the two faces of the loop start acting like N and S poles. A solenoid — a stack of loops sitting side by side — pools all of those contributions together: inside, the field is strong and uniform; outside, it’s a dead ringer for a bar magnet’s field. The grip rule bends to suit: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is where the field lines come pouring out.
mack.17: Three-dimensional fields get squeezed onto flat pages with a handy convention: × for a vector heading into the page and • for one coming out of it. You’ll get plenty of practice with this notation throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
mack.18: Trap The grip rule runs on conventional current — the direction positive charge flows, from the + terminal round through the circuit to the −. Point your thumb along the electron flow instead and you’ll predict a field that’s flipped a full 180° the wrong way.
mack.19: Every field can be sorted along two separate axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold steady over time, and changing if they don’t. A bar magnet sitting still, a fixed charged sphere, a solenoid carrying a steady current — all static. A spinning magnet, or a coil fed alternating current — changing.
mack.20: A field is uniform across a region if it has the same magnitude and the same direction at every point in it — drawn as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It’s non-uniform the moment either one starts wandering from point to point. The field between two oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As shown in FIG 1.4, Earth’s gravitational field is non-uniform once you zoom out to planetary scale, yet inside a single laboratory the lines run so nearly parallel that we happily treat the field as uniform at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
mack.21: Key idea Uniform versus non-uniform is about how the field varies across space; static versus changing is about how it varies over time. The two classifications are completely independent — and every field wears one label from each pair.
mack.22: Connects The static-versus-changing split takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field can induce an emf in a coil. A generator is built for the sole purpose of manufacturing that change.
mack.23: Connects Unit 4 pushes the field idea even further: a changing electric field births a changing magnetic field and vice versa, and the self-sustaining pair goes sailing through empty space as light — a field with no source tied to it at all.

levin.0: Gravity, electricity, magnetism — each can reach across an empty gap and pull or push without ever touching. The field is how. Imagine space itself carrying, at every point, a hidden arrow; place an object there and it feels the force that arrow promises. This topic builds that one idea, and the next four topics lean on it entirely.
levin.1: A field is a region of space where an object feels a force with nothing touching it. Said more carefully: a field is a quantity that has a value at every single point in space. Spread a temperature map across Victoria and you have a scalar field — one bare number at each place. But the three fields of this area of study — gravitational, electric, magnetic — are vector fields: at every point they carry not just a size but a direction too, an arrow waiting in the dark.
levin.2: To read the value at a point, you bring a witness — a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. It must be small, small enough that it listens without speaking, that it does not disturb the very field it has come to measure. Field strength, then, is simply the force the field lays on that test object, counted per unit of whatever property the field acts upon.
levin.3: Each field keeps its own convention for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points along the force the field lays on a test mass — always inward, toward the source mass. The electric field points along the force the field lays on a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points where the north pole of a compass needle comes to rest.
levin.4: That old companion g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ from Units 1&2 was a field strength all along: near Earth's surface, the gravitational force the field lays on each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that, no more and no less. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is defined through the force on a moving charge — the story told in 7 · Magnetic fields.
levin.5: Key idea A field has a value — a magnitude and a direction — at every point in space, whether or not anyone has placed a test object there to feel it. The test object reveals the field; it never creates it. The arrow was already there in the dark.
levin.6: Drawing one arrow at every point would take forever and fill the page to black, so we draw vector fields as field lines instead — continuous curves whose direction, at each point along them, is the field's direction there. Four rules govern every honest field diagram.
levin.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the field that surrounds a point mass and the field that surrounds point charges both fan out radially. Around any point source the same handful of lines must spread across a wider and wider area as you move away, so the lines drift apart and the field grows faint. This thinning is the very root of the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
levin.8: Trap Field lines are a picture, not a thing. A test mass set down in the empty space between two drawn lines still feels a force: the field fills all of space without a gap, and how many lines we choose to draw is only a convention, a choice of the artist.
levin.9: The three fields are kin — each carries a vector at every point, each lays a force on its own kind of object, each shows its strength through how densely the lines crowd together — yet they differ in where they come from, and in whether they can push as well as pull. The table gathers the comparison the study design asks for.
levin.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines: the lines stream away from it, or they stream toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink bound together, the lines running across from one to the other. Gravity has monopoles of a single kind only, for masses can do nothing but attract. Electric charge comes in two signs, so electricity owns both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism owns dipoles alone.
levin.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field reaching out from a bar magnet wear the same shape. The difference hides in what can be torn apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be pulled into two separate monopoles, but cut a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller dipoles — never a single, lonely pole.
levin.12: Trap Cut a bar magnet in half and you do not get one north piece and one south piece. Each half is a whole dipole again, with its own N and its own S — and this goes all the way down, down even to the level of single electrons.
levin.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has only sinks. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism carries both at every point, fused, and never to be parted.
levin.14: Magnetic fields are born from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted watched a compass needle swing aside near a current-carrying wire — the first sign that moving charge conjures a magnetic field. For a sense of scale: Earth's magnetic field in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
levin.15: A bar magnet makes the dipole field of FIG 1.2: lines arcing outside from N to S, crowded thickest at the poles. A straight wire makes circular field lines wrapped around it, rings with no poles at all. To find their direction, reach for the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire in your right hand, thumb laid along the conventional current; your fingers curl the way the magnetic field runs. The rings space themselves wider with distance from the wire, marking the field as it fades.
levin.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment now threads the same way through the centre, so there the field gathers and concentrates; the two faces of the loop take on the roles of N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops set side by side — sums all these contributions: inside, the field runs strong and uniform; outside, it echoes a bar magnet's field. The grip rule bends to suit: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current circles the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is where the field lines step out.
levin.17: Three-dimensional fields must somehow be drawn on flat pages, so we lean on a convention: × for a vector heading into the page, • for one coming out toward you. This notation gets its practice throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
levin.18: Trap The grip rule speaks in conventional current — the direction positive charge would flow, from the + terminal out through the circuit to the −. Lay your thumb along the electron flow instead and you will predict a field turned the wrong way, reversed by a full 180°.
levin.19: Every field can be placed on two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold steady through time, and changing if they do not. A bar magnet sitting still, a charged sphere fixed in place, a solenoid carrying a constant current — all make static fields; a spinning magnet, or a coil fed alternating current, makes a changing one.
levin.20: A field is uniform across a region if it holds the same magnitude and the same direction at every point there — drawn as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It is non-uniform if either one wanders from point to point. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As shown in FIG 1.4, Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform on the scale of the whole planet, and yet within a single laboratory the lines run so nearly parallel that we treat the field as uniform, a steady 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
levin.21: Key idea Uniform and non-uniform describe how a field varies across space; static and changing describe how it varies through time. The two classifications are independent, and every field wears one label from each pair.
levin.22: Connects The static/changing distinction moves to centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field induces an emf in a coil. A generator is built, from the ground up, to manufacture that change.
levin.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, and that to another electric field, and the self-sustaining pair travels out through empty space as light — a field that has cut loose from any source at all.

czerski.0: Gravitational, electric and magnetic forces all manage a curious trick: they push and pull across empty space, with nothing in between to do the shoving. The field model is how we make sense of that. Picture a vector tucked away at every single point in space, and an object dropped at that point simply feels the force the vector hands it. This topic builds the model, and the next four topics put it to work.
czerski.1: A field is a region of space where an object feels a non-contact force. Put more carefully, a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Think of a temperature map of Victoria: one number pinned to each location, hot in Mildura, cooler down in the alps — that’s a scalar field. The three fields in this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are vector fields: at every point the field carries not just a size but a direction too.
czerski.2: To find out what the field is doing at a point, you poke it with a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. The test object has to be small enough that it doesn’t go barging in and disturbing the very field you’re trying to measure. Field strength is then the force the field exerts on the test object, divided by the amount of whatever property the field acts on.
czerski.3: Each field has its own rule for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points the same way as the force the field exerts on a test mass — always inward, toward the source mass. The electric field points the way the force pushes a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points whichever way the north pole of a compass needle swings to face.
czerski.4: That familiar g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ you met back in Units 1&2 is a field strength: near Earth’s surface the gravitational force on each and every kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that, right there in the symbols. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T), and it’s defined through the force on a moving charge — something we tackle in 7 · Magnetic fields.
czerski.5: Key idea A field has a value — a magnitude and a direction — at every point in space, whether or not there’s a test object sitting there to feel it. The test object reveals the field; it doesn’t conjure it into being.
czerski.6: Drawing a separate little arrow at every point would be hopeless — there are infinitely many points — so instead we draw vector fields as field lines: smooth, continuous curves whose direction at any spot is the field’s direction right there. Four rules keep every honest field diagram in line.
czerski.7: As shown in FIG 1.1, the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges fan out radially, like spokes. Around any point source the same set of lines has to spread itself over a bigger and bigger area as you move away, so the lines drift apart and the field thins out. This spreading-out is exactly what sits behind the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
czerski.8: Trap Field lines are a way of drawing the field, not actual objects strung through space. A test mass parked in the gap between two drawn lines still feels a perfectly good force: the field fills every bit of space, and how many lines you choose to draw is just a matter of convention.
czerski.9: The three fields have a family resemblance — a vector at every point, a force on the right kind of object, strength shown by how tightly the lines are packed — but they part ways over where they come from and whether they can push as well as pull. The table lays out the comparison the study design asks for.
czerski.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines: the lines either stream away from it or pour into it, but never both at once. A dipole is a source and a sink working as a pair, with lines arcing across from one to the other. Gravity only ever has monopoles of a single kind, because masses do nothing but attract. Electric charge comes in two flavours, plus and minus, so electricity gets both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism, on the other hand, only ever offers dipoles.
czerski.11: As shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the field reaching out from a bar magnet trace exactly the same shape. The difference is in what you’re allowed to pull apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be separated into two lone monopoles, but saw a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller dipoles — never a single, isolated pole.
czerski.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not leave you holding one north piece and one south piece. Each half turns out to be a complete dipole, with its own N and its own S — and this stubborn behaviour carries right down to the level of individual electrons.
czerski.13: Key idea Field lines stream away from sources and into sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism has both at every point, and you can never prise them apart.
czerski.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted spotted a compass needle twitch as it sat near a current-carrying wire — the first hint that moving charge makes a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, Earth’s magnetic field in Melbourne is around 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT) — gentle enough that it takes a delicate needle to feel it.
czerski.15: A bar magnet produces the dipole field of FIG 1.2: external lines sweeping from N round to S, crowded most tightly at the poles. A straight wire does something different — it produces circular field lines wrapped around the wire, with no poles anywhere. To find which way they go, use the right-hand grip rule: grab the wire in your right hand with your thumb pointing along the conventional current, and your fingers curl the way the magnetic field runs. The circles loosen their spacing as you move away from the wire, showing the field fading.
czerski.16: Bend the wire into a loop and the circular field from every little segment now threads the same way through the middle, so the field there piles up and concentrates; the two faces of the loop start behaving like an N pole and an S pole. The grip rule adapts neatly: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is where the field lines stream out.

It’s non-uniform if either of those wanders from point to point. czerski.21: Key idea Uniform/non-uniform is about how the field varies across space; static/changing is about how it varies over time. The two classifications are entirely independent, and every field picks up one label from each pair.
czerski.22: Connects The static/changing distinction takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field will coax an emf out of a coil. A generator is built from the ground up to manufacture exactly that change.
czerski.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea even further: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, which gives rise to a changing electric field, and on and on — the self-sustaining pair sails off through empty space as light, a field that has cut loose from any source at all.

veritasium.0: Gravity reaches across empty space. So does the pull between charges, and the push between magnets. Nothing touches anything — and yet the force is real. How? Build that one idea now and the next four topics fall into place.

Think of a temperature map of Victoria — one number pinned to each location. That’s a scalar field. veritasium.3: Each field also comes with a direction convention, and it pays to get them straight. The gravitational field at a point points the way the field pushes a test mass — always toward the source mass. The electric field points the way the field pushes a positive test charge.
