attenborough.0: Gravity, electricity, magnetism — three forces that reach across empty space and take hold of an object that nothing is touching. How can this be? The field model is the answer. It tells us that every point in space, however empty it may seem, secretly carries a vector; and any object we place there feels the force that vector commands. Here we shall build that model — the quiet idea on which the next four topics all depend.
attenborough.1: A field is a region of space in which an object feels a force without anything touching it. More carefully still: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every single point in space. Picture a temperature map of Victoria — one number resting at each place. That is a scalar field. But the three fields of this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are richer things. They are vector fields: at every point, the field carries not only a magnitude but a direction too.
attenborough.2: To reveal the value hiding at a point, we send in a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. It must be small — small enough that it does not disturb the very field it has come to measure. Field strength, then, is the force the field exerts on this delicate visitor, taken per unit of whatever property the field acts upon.
attenborough.3: Each field obeys its own convention of direction. The gravitational field at a point points the way the force pushes a test mass — always inward, toward the source mass. The electric field points the way the force pushes a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points wherever the north pole of a compass needle chooses to turn.
attenborough.4: That familiar number from Units 1&2, g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹, is itself a field strength. Near the surface of the Earth, the gravitational force on each kilogram is 9.8 N — and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that, no more and no less. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T), defined through the force on a moving charge, a story we shall follow in 7 · Magnetic fields.
attenborough.5: Key idea A field has a value — magnitude and direction — at every point in space, whether or not a test object is present to feel it. The test object reveals the field; it does not create it.
attenborough.6: To draw a separate arrow at every point would be impossible — there are infinitely many. So instead we draw vector fields as field lines: continuous curves whose direction, at every point along them, is the direction of the field there. Four simple rules govern every correct field diagram.
attenborough.7: Look at FIG 1.1. Around a point mass, and around point charges, the field spreads out in straight radial lines. From any point source the very same set of lines must cover a larger and larger area as we move away, so the lines drift apart and the field grows weaker. Here, quietly, is the origin of the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
attenborough.8: Trap Field lines are a picture, not real objects in space. A test mass set down in the empty gap between two drawn lines still feels a force — for the field fills all of space, and the number of lines we choose to draw is merely a matter of convention.
attenborough.9: The three fields share a common nature — a vector at every point, a force on the right kind of object, and a strength shown by how densely the lines crowd together — yet they differ in where they come from, and in whether they can push as well as pull. The table gathers the comparison the study design asks of us.
attenborough.10: A monopole is a single source, or a single sink, of field lines: the lines stream away from it, or toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink bound together as a pair, the lines running across from one to the other. Gravity, since masses only ever attract, knows monopoles of just one kind. But electric charge comes in two signs — so electricity has both monopoles and dipoles; and magnetism, curiously, has dipoles alone.
attenborough.11: Look now at FIG 1.2. The field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet wear precisely the same shape. The difference lies in what may be torn apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be pulled away into two separate monopoles — but cut a bar magnet in half, and you are left with two smaller dipoles, never a single isolated pole.
attenborough.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not give you one north piece and one south piece. Each half is a complete dipole all its own, with its own N and its own S — and this continues, halving and halving, all the way down to the level of the individual electron.
attenborough.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has sources and sinks. Magnetism has both, at every point, inseparably bound together.
attenborough.14: Magnetic fields are born from two kinds of source: permanent magnets, and electric currents. In 1820 Ørsted chanced to notice a compass needle flick aside near a current-carrying wire — the first hint anyone had seen that moving charge conjures a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, the Earth's own magnetic field here in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
attenborough.15: A bar magnet produces the dipole field of FIG 1.2: lines arcing outside from N to S, crowding densest at the poles. A straight wire produces something quite different — circular field lines, wrapped around the wire, with no poles at all. To find their direction we use the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire in the right hand, thumb pointing along the conventional current, and the fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. Move away from the wire and the circles space out, betraying the field as it weakens.
attenborough.16: Bend the wire into a loop, and the circular field of every segment now threads the same way through the centre, gathering the field there into a strong concentration; the two faces of the loop take on the roles of N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops set side by side — pools all these contributions together: inside, the field grows strong and uniform; outside, it mimics the field of a bar magnet. The grip rule adapts gracefully — curl the fingers of the right hand the way the current runs around the coil, and the thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As FIG 1.3 shows, the N end is where the field lines escape.
attenborough.17: Three-dimensional fields must somehow be drawn on a flat page, and so we use a quiet convention: × for a vector heading into the page, • for a vector emerging out of it. This notation we shall practise throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
attenborough.18: Trap The grip rule uses conventional current — the direction in which positive charge flows, from the + terminal, through the circuit, to the −. Apply it instead with the thumb along the electron flow, and it will predict a field reversed by a full 180°.
attenborough.19: Every field may be classified along two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold perfectly still through time, and changing otherwise. A stationary bar magnet, a fixed charged sphere, a solenoid carrying a steady current — all of these breathe out static fields; but a rotating magnet, or a coil fed alternating current, produces a field forever in flux.
attenborough.20: A field is uniform in a region if it holds the same magnitude and the same direction at every point there — drawn as parallel lines, evenly spaced. It is non-uniform if either of these wanders from point to point. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As FIG 1.4 reveals, the Earth's gravitational field is non-uniform across the whole planet — and yet within a single laboratory the lines run so very nearly parallel that we may treat the field as uniform, at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
attenborough.21: Key idea Uniform/non-uniform describes how a field varies across space; static/changing describes how it varies over time. The two classifications are quite independent — and every field carries one label drawn from each pair.
attenborough.22: Connects The distinction between static and changing rises to centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field can induce an emf in a coil. A generator is built, deliberately, to bring that change about.
attenborough.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, and that to another electric field in turn — and this self-sustaining pair sails away through empty space as light itself, a field with no source left attached to it.

greekepic.0: Sing now of the unseen powers — gravity, and the electric, and the magnetic — that reach across the void and lay hold of things they never touch. The field model unfolds the manner of it: that every point of space bears a vector hidden within it, and whatsoever object is set down at that point feels the answering force. This song raises the model upon which the four songs to come shall stand.
greekepic.1: A field is a region of space wherein an object suffers a force without any touching. More truly told, a field is a quantity that holds a value at every point of space. A map of the warmth of Victoria is a scalar field — one number dwelling at each place. But the three fields of this area of study — gravitational, electric, and magnetic — are vector fields: at every point the field bears both a magnitude and a direction.
greekepic.2: The value at a point is brought to light by a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric, a compass needle for the magnetic. Yet the test object must be small enough that it troubles not the field it would measure. Field strength, then, is the force the field lays upon the test object, reckoned per unit of that property on which the field takes hold.
greekepic.3: Each field keeps its own decree of direction. The gravitational field at a point runs in the direction of the force the field lays upon a test mass — ever toward the source mass. The electric field runs in the direction of the force the field lays upon a positive test charge. And the magnetic field runs in the direction toward which the north pole of a compass needle turns.
greekepic.4: That well-known g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ from Units 1&2 is itself a field strength: near the surface of the Earth, the gravitational force laid upon each kilogram is 9.8 N, and the unit N kg⁻¹ proclaims exactly that. The magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is defined through the force laid upon a moving charge, as is told in 7 · Magnetic fields.
greekepic.5: Key idea A field bears a value — magnitude and direction — at every point of space, whether or not a test object stands there. The test object reveals the field; it does not bring it into being.
greekepic.6: To draw a separate arrow at every point of space is a labour beyond mortal hand, and so vector fields are drawn as field lines — unbroken curves whose direction at every point is the direction of the field there. Four decrees govern every field diagram rightly made.
greekepic.7: As is shown in FIG 1.1, the patterns of the field about a point mass and about point charges are radial. About any point source the selfsame set of lines spreads over an ever greater expanse with distance, so the spacing of the lines widens and the field grows faint. Herein lies the foundation of the inverse-square laws of topics 5 and 6.
greekepic.8: Trap Mark well: field lines are a likeness, not things of substance. A test mass set down between two drawn lines suffers a force all the same: for the field fills the whole of space, and the number of lines drawn is but a matter of convention.
greekepic.9: The three fields share their common nature — a vector at every point, a force laid upon the fitting kind of object, and a strength shown forth by the crowding of the lines — yet they differ in their sources, and in whether they have the power to repel. The table gathers the comparison the study design demands.
greekepic.10: A monopole is a source or a sink of field lines: the lines point away from it, or toward it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink wedded together, with lines running from the one to the other. Gravity owns monopoles of one kind alone, for masses do nothing but draw together. Electric charge comes in two signs, and so electricity owns both monopoles and dipoles; magnetism owns dipoles only.
greekepic.11: As is shown in FIG 1.2, the field of an electric dipole and the outer field of a bar magnet wear the same shape. The difference lies in what may be sundered: the two charges of an electric dipole may be drawn apart into two monopoles, but cleave a bar magnet in two and you bring forth two lesser dipoles, never a pole standing alone.
greekepic.12: Trap To cleave a bar magnet in two gives not one piece of north and one piece of south. Each half is a whole dipole, bearing its own N and S — and this holds true downward even to the level of the single electron.
greekepic.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity owns sinks alone. Electricity owns sources and sinks. Magnetism owns both at every point, never to be parted.
greekepic.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: the permanent magnets and the electric currents. In the year 1820, Ørsted beheld a compass needle swing aside near a wire that bore a current — the first witness that moving charge gives birth to a magnetic field. And for a measure of scale, the magnetic field of the Earth in Melbourne is about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
greekepic.15: A bar magnet brings forth the dipole field of FIG 1.2: outer lines running from N to S, thickest at the poles. A straight wire brings forth circular field lines wheeling about the wire, with no poles at all. The direction is told by the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire with the right hand, the thumb pointing along the conventional current; the fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. The circles spread apart with distance from the wire, showing the field grow faint.
greekepic.16: Bend the wire into a loop, and the circular field of every segment passes the selfsame way through the centre, so that there the field is gathered thick; and the two faces of the loop play the part of N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops set side by side — joins these contributions together: within, the field is strong and uniform; without, it matches the field of a bar magnet. The grip rule bends itself to suit: curl the fingers of the right hand in the direction of the current about the coil, and the thumb points along the field within, toward the N end. As is shown in FIG 1.3, the N end is where the field lines depart.
greekepic.17: Fields of three dimensions are drawn upon flat pages by the convention × for a vector into the page and • for a vector out of the page. This notation is practised throughout 7 · Magnetic fields.
greekepic.18: Trap The grip rule reckons by conventional current — the direction of positive-charge flow, from the + terminal through the circuit to the −. Lay the thumb instead along the flow of the electrons, and it foretells a field direction turned about by 180°.
greekepic.19: Every field may be set upon two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point change not over time, and changing otherwise. A bar magnet at rest, a charged sphere held fast, and a solenoid bearing a steady current all bring forth static fields; a magnet set spinning, or a coil bearing alternating current, brings forth a changing field.
greekepic.20: A field is uniform within a region if it bears the same magnitude and the same direction at every point therein — drawn as lines parallel and evenly spaced. It is non-uniform if either one wavers from point to point. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field about a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As is shown in FIG 1.4, the gravitational field of the Earth is non-uniform upon the scale of the planet, yet within a laboratory the lines run so nearly parallel that the field is taken as uniform at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
greekepic.21: Key idea Uniform and non-uniform tell of variation across space; static and changing tell of variation over time. The two reckonings stand independent, and every field bears one name drawn from each pair.
greekepic.22: Connects The parting of static from changing comes to its full might in Unit 3 AOS 3: for only a changing magnetic field calls forth an emf within a coil. A generator is wrought to bring that change to pass.
greekepic.23: Connects Unit 4 carries the field idea further still: a changing electric field begets a changing magnetic field, and that in turn the electric, and the self-sustaining pair journeys through the empty void as light — a field that bears no source attached to it.

cnpop.0: 引力、电力、磁力，这仨货有个共同的逆天操作：隔着空气、隔着真空，啥都不碰就能把力甩过去。场模型就是来解释这事儿的：空间里的每一个点都揣着一个矢量，你把个东西往那点一放，对应的力就当场招呼上了。这一节我们搭的这套模型，后面整整四节都得靠它吃饭。
cnpop.1: 所谓场，就是一片空间，往里头放个东西，它就会吃到一个不接触的力。说得再讲究点，场是一个物理量，空间里每一个点都给它配了个值。一张维多利亚州的温度地图就是个标量场——每个点就一个数。而这个研究领域里的三大主角——引力场、电场、磁场——全是矢量场：每个点上,场不光有大小，还有方向。
cnpop.2: 想知道某一点上的值是多少？派个测试物去探一探就行：引力场派一个小质量，电场派一个小正电荷，磁场派一根罗盘指针。注意，这个测试物得足够小,小到它不会反过来把要测的场给搅乱了。而场强,就是场对测试物施加的力,再除以场所作用的那个属性的每一个单位。
cnpop.3: 每种场都有自己一套方向的规矩。某一点上的引力场方向，就是场对测试质量施力的方向——永远指向源质量。电场方向，是场对一个正测试电荷施力的方向。磁场方向，则是罗盘指针北极所指的方向。
cnpop.4: 你从1&2单元就混熟了的那个 g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹，其实就是个场强：在地球表面附近，引力对每一千克施加的力是 9.8 N，而 N kg⁻¹ 这个单位说的就是这个事。磁场强度 B 的单位是特斯拉（T），它是通过场对运动电荷施加的力来定义的，这块儿留到 7 · Magnetic fields 再细讲。
cnpop.5: Key idea 不管你放不放测试物，空间里的每一个点上，场都自带一个值——有大小，有方向。测试物只是把场给显形了；可不是它造出来的。
cnpop.6: 每个点都画一根箭头？那不得画到天荒地老，根本不现实。所以矢量场改用场线来画——一条条连续的曲线，曲线上每一点的方向，就是那个点上场的方向。所有画得对的场图，都得守四条规矩。
cnpop.7: 看 FIG 1.1，点质量周围和点电荷周围的场图样,都是辐射状的。任何点源周围，同一组场线随着距离越铺越开，摊到越来越大的面积上，所以线越隔越疏，场也就越来越弱。第5节和第6节那俩平方反比定律，根子就在这儿。
cnpop.8: Trap 场线只是个画法，不是真实存在的东西。你把测试质量塞在两条画出来的线中间，它照样吃力：场是塞满整个空间的，至于画几根线，纯属约定俗成、看心情。
cnpop.9: 这三种场有不少共同点——每个点都是一个矢量，都对相应种类的物体施力，强弱都靠线的疏密来表现——但它们的源头不一样，能不能产生排斥也不一样。下面这张表把研究大纲要求对比的几条全给你列齐了。
cnpop.10: 单极子是场线的源头或归宿：线要么全从它身上往外冒，要么全往它身上扎，不能两头通吃。偶极子则是一个源头和一个归宿配成一对，线从这头跑到那头。引力只有一种单极子，因为质量之间只会相互吸引。电荷有正负两种符号，所以电既有单极子也有偶极子；磁呢，就只有偶极子。
cnpop.11: 看 FIG 1.2，一个电偶极子的场和一根条形磁铁外部的场，形状一模一样。区别在于：能不能拆开。电偶极子的两个电荷能给你拽开成两个单极子，可你把条形磁铁咔嚓切成两半，得到的是两块更小的偶极子，永远逮不到一个孤立的磁极。
cnpop.12: Trap 把条形磁铁切成两半，可不会一半是北极、另一半是南极。每一半都是个完整的偶极子，各自带着自己的 N 和 S——而且这事儿一直能切到单个电子那个层级都管用。
cnpop.13: Key idea 场线从源头出发，奔向归宿。引力只有归宿。电既有源头也有归宿。磁则在每一个点上两样都有，还死活拆不开。
cnpop.14: 磁场有两大来源：永磁体和电流。1820 年，奥斯特注意到罗盘指针一靠近通电导线就偏转——这是史上头一个证据，证明运动的电荷能造出磁场。给你个规模感：墨尔本这地方地球磁场大概是 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T（60 μT）。
cnpop.15: 条形磁铁产生的就是 FIG 1.2 里那个偶极子场：外部场线从 N 到 S，磁极处最密。直导线产生的是以导线为圆心的圆形场线，压根没有磁极。方向靠右手抓握定则来定：右手抓住导线，大拇指顺着常规电流的方向指，四指弯曲的方向就是磁场方向。圆圈随着离导线越远越往外撑开，说明场在变弱。
cnpop.16: 把导线弯成一个环，每一小段产生的圆形场就会朝同一个方向穿过环心，于是环心处的场被攒到一块儿、变浓了；这个环的两个面就分别扮演起 N 极和 S 极。螺线管——一圈圈环挨着排开——把这些贡献叠加到一起：里头，场又强又均匀；外头，活脱脱就是一根条形磁铁的场。抓握定则也跟着变招：右手四指顺着电流绕线圈的方向弯，大拇指就指向内部场的方向，也就是 N 端那头。看 FIG 1.3，场线从哪头钻出来，哪头就是 N 端。
cnpop.17: 三维的场要画到平面纸上，就用这套约定：× 表示矢量扎进纸面，• 表示矢量冒出纸面。这套记号会在 7 · Magnetic fields 里反复练手。
cnpop.18: Trap 抓握定则用的是常规电流——也就是正电荷流动的方向，从 + 极出发，穿过电路，回到 −。要是你脑子一抽用大拇指顺着电子流动的方向去比划，算出来的场方向会整整反掉 180°。
cnpop.19: 每种场都能从两个互不相干的维度上去归类。如果一个场在每个点上的大小和方向都不随时间变，它就是静的，反之就是变的。一块不动的条形磁铁、一个固定的带电球、一个通着恒定电流的螺线管，产生的都是静场；而一块旋转的磁铁，或者一个通着交流电的线圈，产生的就是变场。
cnpop.20: 在某个区域里，如果一个场每一点的大小都一样、方向也都一样，那它在这个区域里就是均匀的——画出来就是一组平行、间距相等的线。要是大小或方向有一个随点变化，那就是非均匀的。两块异号带电平行板之间的场是均匀的；点质量或点电荷周围的辐射状场则是非均匀的。看 FIG 1.4，地球引力场在整个行星尺度上是非均匀的，可在一间实验室那么大的范围里，场线平行得几乎挑不出毛病，于是就当它是均匀的，取 9.8 N kg⁻¹。
cnpop.21: Key idea 均匀／非均匀说的是场在空间上变不变；静／变说的是场随时间变不变。这两套分类互不干涉，每个场都得从这两对标签里各领一个。
cnpop.22: Connects 静／变这个区别，到了 Unit 3 AOS 3 就成了绝对主角：只有变化的磁场才能在线圈里感应出电动势。发电机这玩意儿，就是专门为了制造这种变化才造出来的。
cnpop.23: Connects 到了第4单元，场这个概念还能继续往外炸：一个变化的电场会产生一个变化的磁场，反过来也一样，这对自给自足的搭档穿过空荡荡的真空一路狂飙，那就是光——一种没拖着任何源头的场。

ai.0: Gravity, electricity and magnetism all pull off the same trick: they reach across empty space and push or pull on things they never touch. How? The field model is the answer — the idea that every single point in space quietly carries a vector, and any object you drop at that point feels the matching force. Get this one idea straight and the next four topics fall into place, because they all lean on it.
ai.1: A field is a region of space where an object feels a non-contact force — but let’s sharpen that. A field is really a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Picture a temperature map of Victoria: one number pinned to each spot. That’s a scalar field. The three fields you’ll wrestle with in this area of study — gravitational, electric and magnetic — are richer. They’re vector fields: at every point the field carries both a size and a direction.
ai.2: How do you find the value at a point? You probe it with a test object: a small mass for the gravitational field, a small positive charge for the electric field, a compass needle for the magnetic field. The catch — the test object has to be tiny enough that it doesn’t shove the very field you’re trying to measure. Field strength, then, is the force the field exerts on your test object, taken per unit of whatever property the field acts on.
ai.3: Each field comes with its own rule for which way it points. The gravitational field at a point points the way the field pushes a test mass — always toward the source mass. The electric field points the way the field pushes a positive test charge. And the magnetic field points wherever the north pole of a compass needle swings to.
ai.4: That trusty g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ you met in Units 1&2? It’s a field strength. Near Earth’s surface, every kilogram feels 9.8 N of gravitational pull, and the unit N kg⁻¹ says exactly that — newtons per kilogram. Magnetic field strength B is measured in tesla (T) and is pinned down through the force on a moving charge, which you’ll dig into in 7 · Magnetic fields.
ai.5: Key idea A field has a value — magnitude and direction — at every point in space, test object or no test object. The test object simply reveals the field; it doesn’t conjure it into being.
ai.6: You can’t draw a separate arrow at every point — there are infinitely many. So we draw vector fields as field lines: continuous curves whose direction at any point is the field’s direction right there. Four rules keep every honest field diagram in line.
ai.7: Look at FIG 1.1 — the field patterns around a point mass and around point charges fan out radially. Around any point source, that same bundle of lines is forced to spread over a bigger and bigger area as you move away, so the lines pull apart and the field fades. This simple geometry is the seed of the inverse-square laws you’ll meet in topics 5 and 6.
ai.8: Trap Field lines are a picture, not real objects. Park a test mass in the gap between two drawn lines and it still feels a force — the field fills all of space, and how many lines you choose to draw is just convention.
ai.9: The three fields have plenty in common — a vector at every point, a force on the right kind of object, strength shown by how tightly the lines pack — but they part ways on where they come from and on whether they can push as well as pull. The table lays out the comparison the study design asks you to know.
ai.10: A monopole is a pure source or a pure sink of field lines: the lines either stream away from it or dive into it, but never both. A dipole is a source and a sink paired up, with lines running from one to the other. Gravity only ever has one kind of monopole, because masses only attract — never repel. Electric charge comes in two flavours, so electricity boasts both monopoles and dipoles. Magnetism? Dipoles only.
ai.11: As FIG 1.2 shows, the field of an electric dipole and the field outside a bar magnet wear the same shape. The real difference is what you can pull apart: the two charges of an electric dipole can be separated into two lone monopoles, but slice a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller dipoles — never an isolated pole.
ai.12: Trap Cutting a bar magnet in half does not hand you one north piece and one south piece. Each half is a complete dipole with its own N and S — and that stubborn fact holds all the way down to individual electrons.
ai.13: Key idea Field lines point away from sources and toward sinks. Gravity has sinks only. Electricity has both sources and sinks. Magnetism has both at every point, locked together and impossible to separate.
ai.14: Magnetic fields spring from two kinds of source: permanent magnets and electric currents. Back in 1820, Ørsted spotted a compass needle twitch near a current-carrying wire — the first hint that moving charge brews a magnetic field. For a sense of scale, Earth’s magnetic field in Melbourne runs about 6.0 × 10⁻⁵ T (60 μT).
ai.15: A bar magnet throws out the dipole field of FIG 1.2: external lines arcing from N to S, packed densest at the poles. A straight wire does something different — circular field lines wrapped around the wire, with no poles at all. To find which way they circle, reach for the right-hand grip rule: grip the wire with your right hand, thumb pointing along the conventional current; your fingers curl the way the magnetic field runs. The circles spread apart the further you get from the wire, showing the field weakening.
ai.16: Bend that wire into a loop and the circular field of every segment is funnelled the same way through the centre, so the field there piles up; the two faces of the loop start acting as N and S poles. A solenoid — many loops stacked side by side — adds all these contributions together: inside, the field is strong and uniform; outside, it mimics a bar magnet’s field. The grip rule simply adapts: curl the fingers of your right hand the way the current loops around the coil, and your thumb points along the field inside, toward the N end. As FIG 1.3 shows, the N end is where the field lines burst out.
ai.17: Three-dimensional fields get squashed onto flat pages using a neat convention: × for a vector heading into the page and • for one coming out at you. You’ll get plenty of practice with this notation in 7 · Magnetic fields.
ai.18: Trap The grip rule runs on conventional current — the direction positive charge flows, from the + terminal through the circuit to the −. Point your thumb along the electron flow instead and you’ll predict a field direction flipped a full 180°.
ai.19: Every field can be sorted along two independent axes. A field is static if its magnitude and direction at each point hold steady over time, and changing if they don’t. A stationary bar magnet, a fixed charged sphere and a solenoid carrying a constant current all make static fields; spin that magnet, or run alternating current through that coil, and you get a changing field.
ai.20: A field is uniform across a region if it has the same magnitude and the same direction at every point in it — drawn as parallel, evenly spaced lines. It’s non-uniform if either one drifts from point to point. The field between oppositely charged parallel plates is uniform; the radial field around a point mass or point charge is non-uniform. As FIG 1.4 shows, Earth’s gravitational field is non-uniform on the planetary scale — yet zoom into a laboratory and the lines run so nearly parallel that we happily treat the field as uniform at 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
ai.21: Key idea Uniform/non-uniform is about variation across space; static/changing is about variation over time. The two classifications are independent, and every field wears exactly one label from each pair.
ai.22: Connects The static/changing split takes centre stage in Unit 3 AOS 3: only a changing magnetic field induces an emf in a coil. A generator is built for the express purpose of manufacturing that change.
ai.23: Connects Unit 4 pushes the field idea even further: a changing electric field spawns a changing magnetic field, and vice versa, and that self-sustaining pair sails through empty space as light — a field with no source attached to it at all.
